description: Constitutional and procedural law definitions synthesized from public domain sources: U.S. Supreme Court opinions (public domain per 17 U.S.C. § 105), Bouvier's Law Dictionary (1856), and Anderson's Dictionary of Law (1889). This file replaces an earlier subset that cited a copyrighted commercial edition; all definitions below are derived exclusively from public domain materials.

terms.definition: The guarantee that the government shall not deprive any person of life, liberty, or property without notice and an opportunity to be heard before a tribunal with authority to decide the case. The Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments require both that procedures be fair (procedural due process) and that laws be substantively reasonable (substantive due process).
terms.citation: Mathews v. Eldridge, 424 U.S. 319, at p. 333 (1976); U.S. Const. amend. V, XIV

terms.definition: A quantum of particularized, articulable facts that, taken together with rational inferences, amount to an objective basis for suspecting a person of criminal activity. Sufficient to justify a brief investigatory stop under the Fourth Amendment, but less demanding than probable cause.
terms.citation: Terry v. Ohio, 392 U.S. 1, at p. 21 (1968); United States v. Cortez, 449 U.S. 411, at p. 417 (1981)

terms.definition: A fair probability, based on facts and circumstances known to the officer, that contraband or evidence of a crime will be found in the place to be searched, or that the person to be arrested has committed or is committing an offense. The standard is judged by the totality of the circumstances.
terms.citation: Illinois v. Gates, 462 U.S. 213, at p. 238 (1983); Bouvier's Law Dictionary (1856), p. 382

terms.definition: Notice that the law imputes to a person by reason of facts that would have been discovered upon reasonable inquiry, or by reason of publicly recorded instruments such as deeds. Distinguished from actual notice in that the party need not have actual knowledge.
terms.citation: Bouvier's Law Dictionary (1856), p. 211; Anderson's Dictionary of Law (1889), p. 706

terms.definition: The pretense or appearance of legal authority, especially the semblance of official power exercised by a state actor. Under 42 U.S.C. § 1983, a person acts under color of law when misusing power possessed by virtue of state authority.
terms.citation: Monroe v. Pape, 365 U.S. 167, at p. 184 (1961); United States v. Classic, 313 U.S. 299, at p. 326 (1941)

terms.definition: A formal arrest or restraint of freedom of movement of a degree associated with formal arrest, such that a reasonable person in the suspect's position would not feel free to terminate the encounter and leave. Custodial interrogation triggers Miranda warnings.
terms.citation: Miranda v. Arizona, 384 U.S. 436, at p. 444 (1966); Berkemer v. McCarty, 468 U.S. 420, at p. 440 (1984)

terms.definition: The mental state or criminal intent that the prosecution must prove as an element of most crimes. As established by the common law and the Model Penal Code, the principal culpable mental states are purpose, knowledge, recklessness, and negligence.
terms.citation: Morissette v. United States, 342 U.S. 246, at p. 251 (1952); Bouvier's Law Dictionary (1856), p. 346

terms.definition: The external, physical element of a crime that must concur with a culpable mental state to establish criminal liability. Includes acts, omissions where there is a duty to act, and in some offenses, possession of prohibited items.
terms.citation: Bouvier's Law Dictionary (1856), p. 35; see also Martin v. State, 17 Ala. App. 32 (1919)

terms.definition: Evidence sufficient on its face to establish a presumption or a cause of action unless rebutted. A prima facie case is one that will prevail absent contrary evidence.
terms.citation: Bouvier's Law Dictionary (1856), p. 377; Anderson's Dictionary of Law (1889), p. 803

terms.definition: The obligation incumbent on a party to establish the truth of a claim to the degree required by the applicable standard. In civil cases the ordinary standard is preponderance of the evidence; in criminal cases, proof beyond a reasonable doubt.
terms.citation: Bouvier's Law Dictionary (1856), p. 106; In re Winship, 397 U.S. 358, at p. 364 (1970)

terms.definition: The lawful authority of a court or other governmental body to adjudicate controversies or to exercise power over persons and things within a defined territory. Encompasses both subject-matter jurisdiction and personal jurisdiction.
terms.citation: Bouvier's Law Dictionary (1856), p. 301; International Shoe Co. v. Washington, 326 U.S. 310, at p. 316 (1945)

terms.definition: Constitutional prerequisite for federal court jurisdiction requiring a plaintiff to show (1) a concrete and particularized injury in fact, (2) a causal connection between the injury and the challenged conduct, and (3) a likelihood that a favorable judicial decision will redress the injury.
terms.citation: Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555, at p. 560 (1992); U.S. Const. art. III, § 2

terms.definition: An exemption from legal duty, liability, or legal process. In constitutional law, governmental immunity from suit without its consent (sovereign immunity); immunity of public officials from personal liability for official acts (official immunity).
terms.citation: Bouvier's Law Dictionary (1856), p. 271; Anderson's Dictionary of Law (1889), p. 519

terms.definition: The conferral of discretionary authority by Congress upon an executive agency, which is permissible so long as Congress provides an intelligible principle to guide the agency's exercise of authority. The non-delegation doctrine limits how broadly Congress may confer legislative power.
terms.citation: Whitman v. American Trucking Ass'ns, 531 U.S. 457, at p. 472 (2001); J.W. Hampton Jr. & Co. v. United States, 276 U.S. 394, at p. 409 (1928)

terms.definition: A writ requiring a person detaining another to produce the body of the prisoner before the court, that the court may inquire into the legality of the detention. Preserved by Article I, Section 9 of the Constitution except in cases of rebellion or invasion.
terms.citation: Bouvier's Law Dictionary (1856), p. 251; Ex parte Merryman, 17 F. Cas. 144, at p. 148 (C.C.D. Md. 1861)

terms.definition: The constitutional mandate, found in the Fourteenth Amendment and applied to the federal government through the Fifth Amendment's due process clause, that government may not subject similarly situated persons to unequal treatment without sufficient justification.
terms.citation: Brown v. Board of Education, 347 U.S. 483, at p. 495 (1954); U.S. Const. amend. XIV, § 1

terms.definition: The principle that the Due Process Clauses of the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments protect against arbitrary deprivations of life, liberty, and property, independent of the procedures employed. Legislation infringing a fundamental right is subject to strict scrutiny.
terms.citation: Washington v. Glucksberg, 521 U.S. 702, at p. 719 (1997); Palko v. Connecticut, 302 U.S. 319, at p. 325 (1937)

terms.definition: The guarantee that before the government deprives a person of a protected life, liberty, or property interest, it must afford notice and an opportunity to be heard appropriate to the nature of the case, as determined by balancing private interest, risk of erroneous deprivation, and governmental interest.
terms.citation: Mathews v. Eldridge, 424 U.S. 319, at p. 335 (1976); Goldberg v. Kelly, 397 U.S. 254, at p. 267 (1970)

terms.definition: Government intrusion into an area where a person has a reasonable expectation of privacy, or a physical trespass on a person's effects for the purpose of obtaining information or evidence. The Fourth Amendment requires searches and seizures to be reasonable and ordinarily requires a warrant supported by probable cause.
terms.citation: Katz v. United States, 389 U.S. 347, at p. 351 (1967); U.S. Const. amend. IV

terms.definition: A judicial writ issued upon a showing of probable cause, supported by oath, particularly describing the place to be searched and the persons or things to be seized. Required by the Fourth Amendment except in recognized exigent circumstances.
terms.citation: Bouvier's Law Dictionary (1856), p. 464; U.S. Const. amend. IV

terms.definition: Emergency conditions that excuse the warrant requirement and permit a warrantless search or entry. Recognized categories include hot pursuit of a fleeing felon, imminent destruction of evidence, risk of harm to officers or the public, and emergency aid.
terms.citation: Mincey v. Arizona, 437 U.S. 385, at p. 392 (1978); Payton v. New York, 445 U.S. 573, at p. 590 (1980)

terms.definition: A judicially created remedy that bars the admission of evidence obtained in violation of a defendant's Fourth, Fifth, or Sixth Amendment rights. Its principal purpose is to deter future police misconduct.
terms.citation: Mapp v. Ohio, 367 U.S. 643, at p. 655 (1961); Weeks v. United States, 232 U.S. 383, at p. 393 (1914)

terms.definition: Evidence that is tainted because it was discovered through exploitation of an initial constitutional violation. Such derivative evidence is inadmissible unless the connection between the illegality and the discovery has been attenuated, or the evidence would inevitably have been discovered by lawful means.
terms.citation: Wong Sun v. United States, 371 U.S. 471, at p. 488 (1963); Silverthorne Lumber Co. v. United States, 251 U.S. 385, at p. 392 (1920)

terms.definition: An exception to the exclusionary rule permitting admission of evidence obtained under a search warrant that is later found to be invalid, provided law enforcement officers relied upon the warrant in objectively reasonable good faith at the time of the search.
terms.citation: United States v. Leon, 468 U.S. 897, at p. 922 (1984); Massachusetts v. Sheppard, 468 U.S. 981, at p. 988 (1984)

terms.definition: A defense available to government officials sued in their individual capacities under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 that shields them from liability unless they violated a clearly established statutory or constitutional right of which a reasonable person would have known at the time of the conduct.
terms.citation: Harlow v. Fitzgerald, 457 U.S. 800, at p. 818 (1982); Pearson v. Callahan, 555 U.S. 223, at p. 231 (2009)

terms.definition: The principle, rooted in common law and constitutional structure, that the sovereign cannot be sued without its consent. Congress has partially waived federal sovereign immunity through statutes such as the Federal Tort Claims Act.
terms.citation: Bouvier's Law Dictionary (1856), p. 431; United States v. Mitchell, 463 U.S. 206, at p. 212 (1983)

terms.definition: The clause of the First Amendment providing that Congress shall make no law respecting an establishment of religion. Incorporates against the states via the Fourteenth Amendment. Prohibits government from officially favoring or endorsing a religion.
terms.citation: Everson v. Board of Education, 330 U.S. 1, at p. 15 (1947); U.S. Const. amend. I

terms.definition: The clause of the First Amendment prohibiting Congress from making any law that restricts the free exercise of religion. The government may not substantially burden sincerely held religious beliefs without a compelling interest pursued through narrowly tailored means.
terms.citation: Sherbert v. Verner, 374 U.S. 398, at p. 403 (1963); U.S. Const. amend. I

terms.definition: Article I, Section 8, Clause 3 of the Constitution, granting Congress the power to regulate commerce with foreign nations, among the several states, and with Indian tribes. The primary textual basis for broad federal regulatory authority over economic activity.
terms.citation: Gibbons v. Ogden, 22 U.S. 1, at p. 189 (1824); U.S. Const. art. I, § 8, cl. 3

terms.definition: The negative implication of the Commerce Clause that prohibits states from enacting laws that discriminate against or place undue burdens on interstate commerce, even in the absence of federal legislation on the subject.
terms.citation: Southern Pacific Co. v. Arizona, 325 U.S. 761, at p. 769 (1945); Pike v. Bruce Church Inc., 397 U.S. 137, at p. 142 (1970)

terms.definition: The displacement of state law by federal law under the Supremacy Clause of Article VI. Arises through express preemption (statute explicitly ousts state law), field preemption (federal scheme is so pervasive as to occupy the field), or conflict preemption (state law stands as an obstacle to federal objectives).
terms.citation: McCulloch v. Maryland, 17 U.S. 316, at p. 427 (1819); U.S. Const. art. VI, cl. 2

terms.definition: The constitutional allocation of legislative, executive, and judicial authority to distinct branches of the federal government, designed to prevent the concentration of power and preserve individual liberty. Each branch possesses defined powers and is subject to the checks of the other two.
terms.citation: Marbury v. Madison, 5 U.S. 137, at p. 176 (1803); The Federalist No. 51 (Madison), at p. 321 (Rossiter ed. 1961)

terms.definition: The system of mutual restraints among the three branches of the federal government that prevents any single branch from accumulating excessive power. Each branch has constitutional means to check the actions of the other branches.
terms.citation: Youngstown Sheet & Tube Co. v. Sawyer, 343 U.S. 579, at p. 635 (1952) (Jackson, J., concurring); The Federalist No. 51 (Madison), at p. 320

terms.definition: The power of federal courts to review the constitutionality of legislative and executive acts and to invalidate those that conflict with the Constitution. Established as the prerogative of the federal judiciary in Marbury v. Madison (1803).
terms.citation: Marbury v. Madison, 5 U.S. 137, at p. 177 (1803); Cooper v. Aaron, 358 U.S. 1, at p. 18 (1958)

terms.definition: The doctrine requiring courts to adhere to prior judicial decisions on the same legal question when the same points arise in subsequent litigation. Promotes stability, predictability, and the equal treatment of litigants. Courts may depart from precedent when it is unworkable, has been eroded, or is a remnant of discarded doctrine.
terms.citation: Bouvier's Law Dictionary (1856), p. 441; Planned Parenthood v. Casey, 505 U.S. 833, at p. 854 (1992)

terms.definition: A prior judicial decision that furnishes a rule of law for resolving similar questions of law and fact in subsequent cases. Binding precedent (as opposed to persuasive precedent) must be followed by lower courts within the same jurisdiction under the doctrine of stare decisis.
terms.citation: Bouvier's Law Dictionary (1856), p. 376; Anderson's Dictionary of Law (1889), p. 802

terms.definition: Plural of dictum. Statements in a judicial opinion that are not essential to the decision of the case before the court. Dicta are not binding on lower courts but may carry persuasive authority, particularly when expressed by a court of high rank.
terms.citation: Bouvier's Law Dictionary (1856), p. 224; Anderson's Dictionary of Law (1889), p. 338

terms.definition: A remark or observation in a judicial opinion that is incidental to the decision of the case, not necessary to support it, and therefore not binding as precedent. From the Latin meaning 'something said in passing.'
terms.citation: Bouvier's Law Dictionary (1856), p. 348; Anderson's Dictionary of Law (1889), p. 715

terms.definition: The reason or rationale for a judicial decision; the principle of law upon which the decision rests and which constitutes binding precedent. Distinguished from dicta, which are not essential to the holding.
terms.citation: Bouvier's Law Dictionary (1856), p. 391; Anderson's Dictionary of Law (1889), p. 858

terms.definition: A constitutional doctrine under the Due Process Clause requiring that criminal statutes give persons of ordinary intelligence fair notice of what conduct is forbidden and provide sufficient standards to prevent arbitrary and discriminatory enforcement.
terms.citation: Connally v. General Construction Co., 269 U.S. 385, at p. 391 (1926); Papachristou v. City of Jacksonville, 405 U.S. 156, at p. 162 (1972)

terms.definition: A First Amendment doctrine permitting facial invalidation of a law that, in addition to reaching unprotected expression, sweeps in a substantial amount of constitutionally protected speech. A law may be challenged as overbroad even by a person whose own conduct is unprotected.
terms.citation: Broadrick v. Oklahoma, 413 U.S. 601, at p. 610 (1973); Thornhill v. Alabama, 310 U.S. 88, at p. 97 (1940)

terms.definition: The most exacting level of judicial review in equal protection and substantive due process analysis. A law challenged under strict scrutiny is upheld only if the government demonstrates it serves a compelling governmental interest and is narrowly tailored to achieve that interest.
terms.citation: Korematsu v. United States, 323 U.S. 214, at p. 216 (1944); Adarand Constructors v. Pena, 515 U.S. 200, at p. 227 (1995)

terms.definition: A level of judicial review applied in equal protection cases involving quasi-suspect classifications (e.g., sex, legitimacy). A law survives intermediate scrutiny only if it is substantially related to an important governmental objective.
terms.citation: Craig v. Boren, 429 U.S. 190, at p. 197 (1976); United States v. Virginia, 518 U.S. 515, at p. 533 (1996)

terms.definition: The default, most deferential level of judicial review in equal protection analysis. A law is upheld if it is rationally related to any conceivable legitimate governmental interest. The challenging party bears the burden of negating every conceivable rational basis.
terms.citation: Williamson v. Lee Optical Co., 348 U.S. 483, at p. 488 (1955); FCC v. Beach Communications, 508 U.S. 307, at p. 313 (1993)

terms.definition: A right that is deeply rooted in the nation's history and traditions and implicit in the concept of ordered liberty. Governmental infringement upon a fundamental right triggers strict scrutiny in both due process and equal protection analysis.
terms.citation: Washington v. Glucksberg, 521 U.S. 702, at p. 720 (1997); Griswold v. Connecticut, 381 U.S. 479, at p. 484 (1965)

terms.definition: A categorical distinction among persons — principally race, national origin, or alienage — that triggers strict scrutiny in equal protection analysis because it has historically been used to impose burdens on a discrete and insular minority.
terms.citation: United States v. Carolene Products Co., 304 U.S. 144, at p. 153 n.4 (1938); Brown v. Board of Education, 347 U.S. 483, at p. 495 (1954)

terms.definition: A categorical distinction among persons — principally sex or illegitimacy — that triggers intermediate scrutiny in equal protection analysis. Identified because the characteristic frequently bears no relation to ability to contribute to society.
terms.citation: Craig v. Boren, 429 U.S. 190, at p. 197 (1976); Frontiero v. Richardson, 411 U.S. 677, at p. 686 (1973)

terms.definition: The Fifth Amendment clause providing that private property shall not be taken for public use without just compensation. Applies to direct appropriations (per se takings) and to regulatory measures that deprive an owner of all economically beneficial use of property.
terms.citation: Penn Central Transportation Co. v. City of New York, 438 U.S. 104, at p. 124 (1978); U.S. Const. amend. V

terms.definition: The sovereign power of a government to take private property for public use upon payment of just compensation, exercisable by the federal government under the Fifth Amendment and by the states under equivalent state constitutional provisions.
terms.citation: Bouvier's Law Dictionary (1856), p. 227; Anderson's Dictionary of Law (1889), p. 380; Kelo v. City of New London, 545 U.S. 469, at p. 472 (2005)

terms.definition: The constitutional requirement that a governmental taking of property under eminent domain must serve a public purpose. Modern jurisprudence interprets public use broadly to encompass economic development and other public benefits, not merely literal use by the public.
terms.citation: Kelo v. City of New London, 545 U.S. 469, at p. 480 (2005); Berman v. Parker, 348 U.S. 26, at p. 32 (1954)

terms.definition: The payment the Fifth Amendment requires the government to make when it takes private property under eminent domain. The standard measure is fair market value — the price a willing buyer would pay a willing seller in an arm's-length transaction.
terms.citation: United States v. Miller, 317 U.S. 369, at p. 374 (1943); Olson v. United States, 292 U.S. 246, at p. 255 (1934)

terms.definition: A governmental action that, although stopping short of formal condemnation, goes so far in restricting the use of private property that it is deemed a taking requiring just compensation under the Fifth Amendment. First recognized in Pennsylvania Coal Co. v. Mahon (1922).
terms.citation: Pennsylvania Coal Co. v. Mahon, 260 U.S. 393, at p. 415 (1922); Lucas v. South Carolina Coastal Council, 505 U.S. 1003, at p. 1015 (1992)
